cards.name: Bullish Candle
cards.desc: Price closed above where it opened. Buyers were in control for this candle. The larger the body, the stronger the conviction.
cards.note: Color alone means nothing without context. A bullish candle inside a downtrend is noise. At key support, it is a signal.

cards.name: Bearish Candle
cards.desc: Price closed below where it opened. Sellers were in control for this candle. The larger the body, the stronger the selling pressure.
cards.note: A bearish candle at support in an uptrend is a pullback. The same candle at resistance after a rally is a warning.

cards.name: Marubozu
cards.desc: No wicks. Price opened at one extreme and closed at the other. Pure momentum — no rejection, no hesitation. Whoever was in control, dominated.
cards.note: A bullish marubozu shows total buyer control. A bearish marubozu shows total seller control. Treat these as momentum signals.

cards.name: Doji
cards.desc: Open and close are nearly identical. Neither side won. The market is pausing — waiting. The longer the wicks, the more contested the price.
cards.note: A doji is not a signal. It is a warning. Look left for context before acting. Common variants: Standard, Long-Legged, Dragonfly, Gravestone.

cards.name: Hammer
cards.desc: Long lower wick, small body near the top. Sellers pushed price hard — buyers fought back and closed near the open. The wick is the story. The body color is irrelevant.
cards.note: Must appear after a downtrend or at support. A red hammer is still bullish. Same shape as Hanging Man — location defines meaning.

cards.name: Hanging Man
cards.desc: Long lower wick, small body near the top. Identical shape to the Hammer. At the top of an uptrend, buyers could not hold gains — warning that sellers are entering.
cards.note: Must appear after an uptrend or at resistance. Body color does not define sentiment. Location is everything.

cards.name: Inverted Hammer
cards.desc: Long upper wick, small body near the bottom. Buyers tried to push higher — sellers rejected the move. But the attempt itself signals buyer interest. Needs confirmation.
cards.note: Must appear after a downtrend or at support. A green body does not make it more bullish. Same shape as Shooting Star — location defines meaning.

cards.name: Shooting Star
cards.desc: Long upper wick, small body near the bottom. Buyers pushed price up hard — sellers rejected the move and closed near the open. A green body does not make this bullish.
cards.note: Must appear after an uptrend or at resistance. Same shape as Inverted Hammer — location is everything.

cards.name: Shadows & Wicks
cards.desc: The thin lines above and below the candle body. Upper wick = buyers pushed price here but got rejected. Lower wick = sellers pushed price here but got rejected. Wicks show the battlefield.
cards.note: Long wicks at key levels are high-value information. They show where price was refused — and often where it will be refused again.

cards.name: Engulfing Pattern
cards.desc: A candle that completely engulfs the prior candle's body. Bullish engulfing: large green candle swallows prior red. Bearish engulfing: large red candle swallows prior green. One side overwhelmed the other.
cards.note: Most powerful at key levels after an extended move. The larger the engulf, the stronger the signal. Volume confirms.

cards.name: Harami Pattern
cards.desc: A small candle contained entirely within the prior candle's body. The large candle sets the range — the small candle shows momentum is slowing. A pause, not a reversal.
cards.note: Weaker than the engulfing. Needs confirmation from the next candle. Bullish harami after downtrend, bearish harami after uptrend.

cards.name: Kicker Pattern
cards.desc: Two candles in opposite directions with a gap between them. The strongest single reversal signal in candlestick analysis. Represents a complete shift in sentiment — often driven by news.
cards.note: Rare but powerful. Bullish kicker: red candle followed by green that gaps above. Bearish kicker: green followed by red that gaps below.

cards.name: Morning Star
cards.desc: Three candles: a large bearish candle, a small indecision candle, then a large bullish candle closing above the midpoint of the first. A reversal pattern that signals seller exhaustion.
cards.note: The middle candle is the pivot — the exact moment selling momentum ran out. The third candle is the confirmation.

cards.name: Evening Star
cards.desc: Three candles: a large bullish candle, a small indecision candle, then a large bearish candle closing below the midpoint of the first. The mirror of Morning Star.
cards.note: Most reliable at significant resistance after an extended uptrend. The third candle must close well into the body of the first.

cards.name: Three White Soldiers
cards.desc: Three consecutive bullish candles, each opening within the prior body and closing progressively higher. Strong, sustained buying pressure across three sessions.
cards.note: After a downtrend, this pattern signals a genuine shift. After a long uptrend, be cautious — exhaustion can follow.

cards.name: Three Black Crows
cards.desc: Three consecutive bearish candles, each opening within the prior body and closing progressively lower. The mirror of Three White Soldiers. Sustained, relentless selling.
cards.note: Most reliable after an extended uptrend. Each candle should have a small or no lower wick — showing sellers held control all session.

cards.name: Piercing / Dark Cloud
cards.desc: Two-candle reversal patterns. Piercing: bearish candle followed by bullish that closes above the midpoint of the first (bullish). Dark Cloud Cover: opposite direction (bearish).
cards.note: The key requirement: the second candle must close above (or below) the 50% midpoint of the first candle's body. Less than 50% is just noise.

cards.name: Double Top
cards.desc: Price reaches the same high twice and fails to break through. Two rejections at resistance. The neckline break confirms the pattern and signals a reversal lower.
cards.note: Measure the distance from tops to neckline — that is the projected target after the break. Volume typically decreases on the second top.

cards.name: Double Bottom
cards.desc: Price tests the same low twice and holds. Two rejections at support. The neckline break to the upside confirms the reversal.
cards.note: The mirror of Double Top. The second bottom ideally holds slightly higher than the first, showing less selling pressure.

cards.name: Triple Top
cards.desc: Three failed attempts to break through the same resistance level. Each failure adds weight to the rejection. When the neckline breaks, the move is typically decisive.
cards.note: More reliable than a double top because of the third confirmation. The longer price spends forming the pattern, the more significant the eventual break.

cards.name: Head & Shoulders
cards.desc: Three peaks: a smaller left shoulder, a higher head, a smaller right shoulder. The neckline connects the two troughs. Break below neckline signals reversal.
cards.note: One of the most recognized and reliable reversal patterns. Volume typically peaks on the left shoulder and declines on the head and right shoulder.

cards.name: Rounding Top
cards.desc: A gradual, curved peak showing slow distribution over time. Unlike sharp reversals, this pattern develops slowly as buyers lose control incrementally.
cards.note: Common in longer timeframes. The slow curve reflects institutional distribution — smart money selling into strength over weeks or months.

cards.name: Bull Flag
cards.desc: A sharp upward move (the pole) followed by a controlled, downward-sloping consolidation (the flag). The flag is a pause — buyers catching breath before the next leg up.
cards.note: The tighter and more orderly the flag, the cleaner the breakout. Volume should decrease during the flag and expand on the breakout.

cards.name: Bear Flag
cards.desc: A sharp downward move followed by a controlled, upward-sloping consolidation. The consolidation is a dead-cat bounce — sellers regrouping before continuing lower.
cards.note: Mirror of Bull Flag. The upward drift in the flag is sellers allowing a relief bounce. When it breaks down, the move is often sharp.

cards.name: Pennant
cards.desc: A sharp move followed by a symmetrical triangle consolidation — converging trendlines forming a small symmetrical triangle. Price coils tighter before breaking in the direction of the original move.
cards.note: Similar to a flag but with converging lines rather than parallel. The breakout direction almost always matches the initial move.

cards.name: Wedge
cards.desc: Two converging trendlines both moving in the same direction. Rising wedge (bearish): higher highs and higher lows compressing upward. Falling wedge (bullish): lower highs and lower lows compressing downward.
cards.note: Rising wedge breaks down. Falling wedge breaks up. The longer the wedge, the more powerful the eventual break.

cards.name: Ascending Triangle
cards.desc: Flat resistance at the top, rising support below. Buyers keep making higher lows while sellers defend a fixed level. Pressure builds — when resistance breaks, the move is typically strong.
cards.note: The flat top is where sellers are positioned. Each time buyers make a higher low, sellers weaken. A clean break above resolves the standoff.

cards.name: Descending Triangle
cards.desc: Flat support at the bottom, declining resistance above. Sellers keep making lower highs while buyers defend a fixed level. Mirror of Ascending Triangle.
cards.note: The flat bottom is where buyers are positioned. Each lower high shows sellers are more aggressive. A clean break below resolves the standoff.

cards.name: Symmetrical Triangle
cards.desc: Converging trendlines with lower highs and higher lows. Neither buyers nor sellers are winning. Price coils — energy builds. The breakout direction is the answer.
cards.note: Do not predict the direction. Wait for the break. Volume contraction during formation, expansion on the break is the ideal setup.

cards.name: Support & Resistance
cards.desc: Support: a price level where buying has historically overwhelmed selling. Resistance: where selling has historically overwhelmed buying. These levels exist because traders remember them.
cards.note: Once broken, support becomes resistance and resistance becomes support. This is called a role reversal — one of the most reliable concepts in price action.

cards.name: Gap
cards.desc: When price opens significantly higher or lower than the prior close, leaving an unfilled area on the chart. Gaps represent a sudden shift in sentiment — often from news or overnight events.
cards.note: Gaps have a statistical tendency to fill. Gap N Go: price continues in the gap direction. Gap Fill: price reverses to close the gap. Context determines which.

cards.name: Trend vs Range
cards.desc: Trend: price making consistent higher highs and higher lows (uptrend) or lower highs and lower lows (downtrend). Range: price oscillating between defined support and resistance without direction.
cards.note: The most important question before every trade: is this market trending or ranging? The wrong answer leads to the wrong setup.
